message:
fix(abi): add pipe+fcntl fallback for popen() on kernels without pipe2

The `popen()` entry point in `crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/stdio_abi.rs`
previously reached for the `pipe2` syscall unconditionally and
unconditionally returned the underlying errno on failure, even when
that errno was `ENOSYS`. Very old Linux kernels (and some
seccomp-filtered sandboxes) do not expose `pipe2`, so a guest program
that called `popen("...", "r"|"w")` on such a host would fail during
its first attempt to open a subprocess stream even though the host
absolutely could provide the required pipe semantics via the
legacy `pipe` + `fcntl(F_SETFD, FD_CLOEXEC)` combo.

This commit adds exactly that fallback in the popen pipe-setup path:

1. Keep the fast path: call `pipe2` with `O_CLOEXEC` when the caller
   asked for close-on-exec. Non-`ENOSYS` errors still bail out
   immediately with the original errno, exactly as before.

2. On `ENOSYS`, fall through and call the plain `pipe` syscall.
   If that also fails, we propagate its errno and bail (matching the
   original error-handling shape: `set_abi_errno`, a
   `runtime_policy::observe(ApiFamily::Stdio, ..., observed=true)`
   call, and `ptr::null_mut()`).

3. When the caller requested close-on-exec, set `FD_CLOEXEC` on
   both ends of the pipe via two `fcntl(F_SETFD, FD_CLOEXEC)`
   syscalls. Each fcntl is guarded: on failure we close *both* pipe
   ends before returning null, so we never leak half-open file
   descriptors into the guest on the error path. Errors from the
   fcntls feed through the same `set_abi_errno` /
   `runtime_policy::observe` / `null_mut()` triple used by the
   pipe-open failure branches so observability stays consistent.

No behavior change on any kernel that already supports `pipe2`
(which is every mainstream distro from ~2008 onward); the fallback
only fires on the explicit `ENOSYS` branch. Net change: +58/-4
lines in stdio_abi.rs.
